start.question: What does the insect look most similar to?

bug.name: Bagworm
bug.description: 3/4 to 1 inch, 6 legs, attennae, wings only when male and fully grown into moth. Thick, slate-gray or brown bodies that are tube-shaped and covered in fine hairs. Typically remain inside of silken “bags” that may just resemble a pile of leaves or twigs.
affectsPlants: Evergreens: Juniper, arborvitae, cedar, cypress, pine, spruce, and hemlock
Deciduous Trees/Shrubs: Maple, oak, sycamore, willow, elm, birch, black locust, honeylocust, basswood, poplar, and persimmon
eats: Foliage, generally evergreens, leaving plants weak. Could cause plant death.

bug.name: Oleader Caterpillar
bug.description: 1 to 1.5 inches long, 6 legs, antennae and wings when fully grown. Bright orange, covered in black spots and black hairs. Generally lives in tropical climates.
eats: Feeds on the leaves and stems of oleander plants, which contain very toxic chemicals to humans, which could lead to cardiac arrest. They can trigger allergic reactions if you come into contact with them. They will damage and kill ornamental plants in your garden over time.

bug.name: Fall Web Worm
bug.description: 1 to 1.5 inches long, 6 legs, atennae, and have wings when fully grown. Green/yellow with small orange or red dots, and long tuffs of fuzz. Live in areas with warm or mild climate and high levels of food.
eats: Feeds on foliage, and can damage plants, but not in any serious way. Its more cosmetic, since it happens late in summer. They are known for making large, distinctive webs. In the larval stage they feed in large groups.

bug.name: Grub Worms
bug.description: 1 to 1.5 inches long, 6 legs, no antennae, wings as adults. They are white, slightly translucent, c-shaped, and darker at their head. They are found underground in soil, in lawns, gardens, etc.
eats: They damage roots, leading to damage in turf grass, so check for brown spots in your lawn. Harmless to humans

bug.name: Eastern Tent Caterpillar
bug.description: 2 to 2.5 inches, 6 legs, wings when grown into moths, they have black bodies with red and yellow stripes, with fuzz on their sizes. They live in forests and woodlands.
eats: Not much of a threat to gardens, but can defoiliate trees, but generally don't affect tree health. They create silk tents, active during may and june

bug.name: Fall Armyworm
bug.description: 1.5 inches long, 8 pairs of legs, claw like near the head, wings and attenae in moth form. They are green, brown, black, and a 'Y' mark on their head and three stripes of white on their back. Live in warm climate with lots of foliage, often in agricultural fields.
eats: harmful to crops, especially for farmers, and can happen in a short period of time.

bug.name: Tropical Sod Web Worm
bug.description: 3/4 to 1 inch long, 6 legs, no antennae, wings in moth form. Distinct segments and ridges, light brown and darker heads. They live in warm climates and high humidity, in lawns or places with turfgrass
eats: Destructive to grass, leading to brown patches.

bug.name: Azalea Caterpillar
bug.description: 1.5 to 2 inches, 6 legs, wings in moth stage. They are covered in a thin amount of fine hairs. They are black with yellow stripes and red head. They live in areas with azaleas
eats: Eat in large groups, damaging bushes and leave plants dead.

bug.name: Asian Lady Beetle
bug.description: 6 to 10 mm with 6 legs, antennae, and wings. Yellow to a dark reddish-orange color and has spots on the wing cover. They can be found in crops, attics and crevises
eats: They can bite, however bites do not greatly injure humans. They eat crop pests, making them beneficial to a garden, up to 65 aphids a day. They can become issues in homes.

bug.name: Ground Beetle
bug.description: 1.5 to 25 mm and has 6 legs, antennae, and wings. Dark black or brown, slightly flat and elongated. Often lives in soil, under rocks, logs and leaves.
eats: Is not dangerous to humans. These beetles eat tons of garden pests.

bug.name: Varied Carpet Beetle
bug.description: 2 to 3 mm with 6 legs, antennae and wings. They are oval and dark in the center, with a irregular pattern of white, brown and yellow scales. They live in dark, secluded areas, in nests, trees, and homes, specifically rugs, wall hangings, etc.
eats: Harmless to gardens, only feeding on pollen and nectar. They are minor pollinators. They can be household pests however.

bug.name: Black Carpet Beetle
bug.description: 2.8 to 5 mm with 6 legs, antennae and wings. They are dark black or brown, even color, longer than they are wide. They live in soil, under rocks, logs or leaves.
eats: Harmless to garden, feeding on pollen and nectar from flowers. They can be major pests in your home, damaging fabrics, food and laying eggs.

bug.name: Cigarette Beetle
bug.description: 2 to 3 mm with 6 legs, antennae and wings. They are small, oval shaped with shiny reddish brown scales. They live in warmer climates and do not live in the wild, mostly in human made environments, and specifically in warehouses with tobacco
eats: They do not have an impact on a garden, as they mostly exist in man made areas. They are major pests in homes, particularly for dried vegtables, tobacco, and books

bug.name: North American Wheel Bug
bug.description: 1.5 inches with long legs, antennae and wings. They are grey to brownish gray and have bright red abdomens. They live in gardens and woodlands, staying in leafy areas and hiding, in sunflowers, cotton, trees, fruits and groves.
affectsPlants: beetles, such as the japanese beetle
eats: Beneficial for garden, as it is a predator of common garden pests, however also preys on other beneficial species such as lady bugs. They will defend themselves if they feel threatened, and the bite of an assasin bug is painful, being worse than wasps and longer lasting. It has been documented as extremely painful and can have numbness for days.

bug.name: Milkweed Assassin Bug
bug.description: 0.5 inches with long legs, antennae and wings. Bright red and jet black, and longer than wide. Generally live in gardens or woodlands on and around plants
eats: Beneficial for garden, as it is a predator of common garden pests. They will defend themselves if they feel threatened, and the bite of an assasin bug is not pleasant, leading to a small itchy bump.

bug.name: Green Peach Aphid
bug.description: 2 mm long, with antennae, cornicles and some have wings. They are yellow green, pale green or pinkish and pear shaped. They live in the underside of leaves, going between fruit trees and herbaceous plants between winter and summer
eats: They suck plant sap, causing yellowing, wilting, leaf curling, and stunted growth, destroying many crops. Also are a vector for plant disease. They do not bite humans

bug.name: Black Bean Aphid
bug.description: 2 mm with antennae, cornicles, and some with wings. They are small, soft, and dark green or blackish and legs are pale yellow with black tips. They are found in the undersides of leaves, in various agricultural crops and ornamental plants
eats: They suck plant sap, causing yellowing, wilting, leaf curling, and stunted growth, destroying many crops. Also are a vector for plant disease. They do not bite humans

bug.name: Oleander Aphid
bug.description: 1.5 to 2.6 mm long, with antennae, cornicles and some have wings. They have black and yellow bodies. They live in the underside of leaves, going between fruit trees and herbaceous plants between winter and summer
eats: They suck plant sap, causing yellowing, wilting, leaf curling, and stunted growth, destroying many crops. Also are a vector for plant disease. They do not bite humans

bug.name: Brown Garden Snail
bug.description: 1 inch, 2.5 cm, and is brown and grey. Found most active at night and when its damp, found in soil and around plants.
eats: Feeds on fruit and leaves, leaving circular chewed areas

bug.name: Tropical Leatherleaf
bug.description: slug, 7 or 8 cm, round, dark brown, no shell. Found most active at night and when its damp, found in soil and around plants.
eats: Feeds on fruit and leaves, leaving circular chewed areas

bug.name: Rosy Wolfsnail
bug.description: 7-10 cm, and has a light grey or brown body. Found most active at night and when its damp, found in soil and around plants.
eats: They eat other snails and slugs, typically ones that are smaller than themselves
